quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Centripetal force"?

options.text: r⊥ = r sinθ; the perpendicular distance from the pivot point to the line of action of the force
F_c = mv²/r; the net radial force needed to maintain circular motion; always directed toward the center
s = rθ; the arc length swept by a point at radius r when the object rotates through angle θ (in radians)
v(t) = dx/dt; the derivative of position with respect to time, giving the rate and direction of motion at each instant

quiz_questions.explanation: "Centripetal force" is defined as: F_c = mv²/r; the net radial force needed to maintain circular motion; always directed toward the center. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Uniform circular motion"?

options.text: s = rθ; the arc length swept by a point at radius r when the object rotates through angle θ (in radians)
F_c = mv²/r = mrω²; the net radial force required to maintain circular motion at a given radius and speed
ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ - θ₀) for constant angular acceleration
Circular motion at constant speed; acceleration is purely centripetal (inward) with magnitude v²/r

quiz_questions.explanation: "Uniform circular motion" is defined as: Circular motion at constant speed; acceleration is purely centripetal (inward) with magnitude v²/r. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Non-uniform circular motion"?

options.text: Circular motion with changing speed; has both centripetal (radial) and tangential acceleration components
P = dW/dt = F·v; the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred, measured in watts (W) = joules/second
ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ - θ₀) for constant angular acceleration
Friction converts kinetic energy to thermal energy; work done by friction equals -f_k × d, always negative

quiz_questions.explanation: "Non-uniform circular motion" is defined as: Circular motion with changing speed; has both centripetal (radial) and tangential acceleration components. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Banked curve"?

options.text: A uniform spherical shell attracts external masses as if all its mass were at the center; inside the shell, gravitational force from the shell is zero
An inclined road surface where the normal force has a radial component, providing centripetal force without relying solely on friction
The vector sum of all forces acting on an object; determines the direction and magnitude of acceleration via F_net = ma
ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ-θ₀); for constant α

quiz_questions.explanation: "Banked curve" is defined as: An inclined road surface where the normal force has a radial component, providing centripetal force without relying solely on friction. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Circular Motion, which best defines "Centripetal acceleration"?

options.text: f_k = μ_k N; the friction force during sliding, proportional to the normal force; typically μ_k < μ_s
a_c = v²/r = rω²; directed toward the center of the circular path; caused by the net inward force
The energy required to completely separate a system; equals |E_total| for a gravitationally bound system
Conversion of mechanical energy to thermal energy by friction: ΔE_thermal = f_k × d

quiz_questions.explanation: In Circular Motion, "Centripetal acceleration" refers to: a_c = v²/r = rω²; directed toward the center of the circular path; caused by the net inward force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F_c = mv²/r; the net radial force needed to maintain circular motion; always directed toward the center"?

options.text: Internal vs. external forces
Binding energy
Centripetal force
Gravitational field

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Centripetal force".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Circular motion at constant speed; acceleration is purely centripetal (inward) with magnitude v²/r"?

options.text: Uniform circular motion
Vector
Apparent weight
Elastic collision

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Uniform circular motion".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Circular motion with changing speed; has both centripetal (radial) and tangential acceleration components"?

options.text: Conservation of mechanical energy
Instantaneous power
Non-uniform circular motion
Energy dissipation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Non-uniform circular motion".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An inclined road surface where the normal force has a radial component, providing centripetal force without relying solely on friction"?

options.text: Total orbital energy
Gravitational PE (near Earth)
Work-Energy Theorem
Banked curve

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Banked curve".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Circular Motion, which term matches: "a_c = v²/r = rω²; directed toward the center of the circular path; caused by the net inward force"?

options.text: Net force
Centripetal acceleration
Velocity of center of mass
Tangential velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: In Circular Motion, this refers to "Centripetal acceleration".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Circular Motion is accurate?

options.text: Friction and Normal Forces describe contact forces that resist relative motion between surfaces.
Circular Motion occurs when an object follows a curved path, requiring a centripetal (center-seeking) acceleration a_c = v²/r directed radially inward.
Newton's Second Law, F_net = ma (or more precisely F_net = dp/dt), relates the net force on an object to its acceleration.
Conservation of angular momentum explains phenomena from spinning ice skaters to the formation of galaxies.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Circular Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Circular Motion?

options.text: Applying Newton's Second Law in the radial direction: ΣF_radial = mv²/r = mrω².
Power and Efficiency describe the rate of energy transfer and the fraction of input energy converted to useful output.
A force is conservative if the work it does depends only on initial and final positions, not the path.
The normal force N adjusts to maintain contact and is not always equal to mg—it depends on the geometry and other applied forces.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Circular Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Circular Motion?

options.text: Work and Energy provide a scalar framework for analyzing motion without tracking forces and accelerations over time.
Friction and Normal Forces describe contact forces that resist relative motion between surfaces.
Torque about a pivot determines whether an object rotates and in which direction.

The centripetal force is not a new type of force—it is the net radial component of real forces (tension, gravity, normal force, friction) that provides the inward acceleration needed to maintain circular motion.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Circular Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Circular Motion?

options.text: Wave properties—wavelength λ, frequency f, speed v = λf, and amplitude—are described mathematically by y(x,t) = A sin(kx - ωt), linking oscillation parameters to wave propagation.
Circular Motion occurs when an object follows a curved path, requiring a centripetal (center-seeking) acceleration a_c = v²/r directed radially inward.
The parallel axis theorem I = I_cm + Md² relates the moment of inertia about any axis to that about the center of mass.
Orbital Motion applies Newton's Second Law with gravitational force to derive orbital parameters.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Circular Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Circular Motion?

options.text: Force-potential energy diagrams reveal equilibrium positions (where F = -dU/dx = 0) and their stability (slope determines restoring or repelling character).
Newton's Second Law in its most general form states F_net = dp/dt, which reduces to F = ma when mass is constant.
Applying Newton's Second Law in the radial direction: ΣF_radial = mv²/r = mrω².

Torque and Rotational Dynamics extend Newton's Second Law to rotation: τ_net = Iα, where torque τ = r × F (magnitude rF sinθ) is the rotational analog of force, and I (moment of inertia) is the rotational analog of mass.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Circular Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Circular Motion?

options.text: Elevator engineering calculating cable tension and motor force requirements for different loading conditions using F = ma for the elevator-passenger system
Engineering centrifuge design calculating the g-forces (a_c = rω²) experienced by samples in medical and materials science applications
Highway engineering designing banked curves at optimal angles so vehicles can navigate turns safely at design speed without relying on friction
Musical instrument design: string tension and density determine wave speed and thus the fundamental frequency of vibrating strings

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Circular Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Circular Motion?

options.text: Calculating energy requirements for elevators and conveyor systems using work against gravity W = mgh
Calculating the angular deceleration of a flywheel when braking force is applied to determine stopping time and number of revolutions
Centrifuge design in molecular biology calculating the centripetal acceleration needed to separate cellular components by density
Automobile engine specifications using power curves (horsepower vs. RPM) to match engine performance to vehicle weight and desired acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Circular Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Circular Motion applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Designing anti-lock braking systems (ABS) that maintain static friction (which exceeds kinetic) by preventing wheel lockup during emergency stops
Amusement park ride engineering ensuring loop-the-loop roller coasters maintain sufficient speed at the top for the normal force to remain positive
Automotive airbag design increasing collision time to reduce peak force on occupants while the same impulse (momentum change) occurs
Wind turbine design optimizing blade angular velocity to maximize energy capture while maintaining safe centripetal acceleration at blade tips

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Circular Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Circular Motion?

options.text: Highway engineering designing banked curves at optimal angles so vehicles can navigate turns safely at design speed without relying on friction
SpaceX and NASA calculating orbital insertion burns using v = √(GM/r) to place satellites at specific altitudes and orbital velocities
Automotive engineering designing braking systems by analyzing deceleration forces and stopping distances using F = ma
Grandfather clock design using the precise period T = 2π√(L/g) of a pendulum to regulate timekeeping mechanisms

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Circular Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Circular Motion in practice?

options.text: Hard drive engineering specifying angular velocity (7200 RPM) and computing read/write head linear speed at different radii
Centrifuge design in molecular biology calculating the centripetal acceleration needed to separate cellular components by density
Hydroelectric dam design converting gravitational PE of water (mgh) to kinetic energy of turbines to electrical energy, analyzing efficiency at each conversion stage
Navigation and GPS systems using vector addition to compute resultant displacement from multiple legs of a journey

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Circular Motion.
